key_insight: Using an LLM to generate Alloy as an intermediate formal specification can expose constraint-level bugs in production software that direct test generation may miss.
engineering_relevance: For software-intensive engineering workflows, this suggests a practical path to convert requirements or source code into executable tests that improve defect detection beyond coverage alone.
method: LLM generates Alloy specifications from README requirements or from production source code.
Executable tests are then derived from the Alloy model and run on real Python libraries.
A direct LLM-only test-generation baseline is compared against the Alloy-mediated pipeline.
evidence_signal: On Flipper and Cerberus, the LLM produced usable Alloy specs and tests without manual correction; the Alloy-based pipeline found a real duplicate-flag bug in Flipper that the direct baseline missed across three runs.
watch_item: Results come from only two open-source libraries, so the apparent benefit of code-derived specifications and reduced variance may not generalize to other systems or specification styles.
paper_title: LM2Alloy: Investigating LLM-Generated Formal Specifications for Automated Test Derivation in Production Software
